div.head: The Consul General at Shanghai (Cabot) to the Secretary of State

opener.dateline:
Shanghai, January 22, 1948—7 p. m.
[Received January 22—6:44 a. m.]

div.p: 156. Local Legislative Yuan electionsFor results of elections, January 21–23, see airgram No. A–130, May 8, from the Ambassador in China, p. 226. appear as completely prearranged and controlled by Govt political leaders as were NPANational People’s Assembly. elections, with popular cynicism and lack of interest even more evidenced. Most noteworthy difference is fact that whereas previous elections were almost entirely a CC affair managed by Pan Kung-chanKuomintang official. with CC taking majority of seats and largely dictating proportional distribution of balance to other Govt factions and minor parties, present elections reveal control now divided between principal rightist groups. San Min Chu I Youth Corporation [Corps], Hsuan Tieh-Wu’sLt Gen. Hsuan Tieh-wu, Garrison Commander of Shanghai–Woosung area. garrison headquarters, BISKuomintang Bureau of Investigations and Statistics. secret police, et cetera, dissatisfied with CC’s deal in previous elections, have since built up their election machines and assured themselves larger share of spoils. Reliable source reports that, with view to impressing foreign opinion, W. W. YenState Councilor of Chinese National Government, former Premier. against his wishes is being elected by order of Generalissimo.
Elections in Shanghai are marked by more disorders (though of minor character) than accompanied previous elections, which is believed attributable to new multifaction basis of contest.
Preliminary information from provinces indicates that CC domination still effective in Kiangsu and Chekiang (richest territory which CC regards as its preserve).
